issues: The numbered cards (01–06) are the primary sequence signal, but no explicit forward-direction arrow or connective text appears between cards — a first-time visitor must infer the sequence from the numbers alone. The header copy ('each one builds on the last') is present but small.

'Waitlist' on cards 02–06 reads ambiguously: a visitor unfamiliar with the school might not know whether 'Waitlist' means 'pay now and wait' or 'express interest for a future cohort.' No tooltip, link, or note clarifies this.
The 'Planned / Waitlist Open' status indicator on specialist track pages uses different language than 'Waitlist' on the course cards — two terms for the same concept erodes confidence.

Price architecture is present but fragmented: $299 founding price appears in the banner, on the card, and on the foundations page, but the $2,499 full-ladder price only appears in the top-cap and in the footer footnote of the After-the-Ladder section. A visitor scanning the cards would not encounter it without reading the container cap copy.

The meta-label 'Gradual Release', 'Experiential / Contrastive Learning' etc. on the course cards adds pedagogical credibility but introduces jargon that is unexplained at the listing level. Useful for initiates; opaque to a first-time visitor.
strengths: The single-container design for the Practitioner Ladder (top cap + cards + bottom destination cap) is structurally sound. The caps bracket the sequence visually.
The Prosthetic Cognition bottom cap correctly names the destination and links to the paper — gives the sequence a terminal point, not just an open list.
The homepage domain-grid (01–06 + arrow to Prosthetic Cognition) mirrors the courses page sequence, reinforcing continuity across the two primary pages.
Foundations is clearly distinguished from the rest: 'Enrolling' vs 'Waitlist', different price, explicit founding-cohort framing. Entry point is unambiguous.

The intro paragraph on courses.html ('start with Foundations, which gives you three structural moves you can apply the same day') is the right orientation text. It answers 'where do I begin' before the visitor reaches the cards.

issues: The prerequisite (full ladder required) appears in the section subhead on courses.html but is not visually emphasized — it sits in small muted text and could be skimmed. A visitor who jumps directly to a track page from search sees it only in a two-line footer note after the entire module breakdown.

The Copywriter track has no dedicated page (copywriter-track.html linked from courses.html but not audited here — likely stubbed or missing). If the file is empty or missing, this is a broken link for a live listing.

Creative Director and Educator tracks have significantly thinner course-listing descriptions on courses.html compared to Brand Designer, Copywriter, Fiction Writer, and Nonfiction Writer. Creative Director gets one sentence; Educator gets one sentence with no substantive content description. At the listing level, these two read as placeholder-tier compared to the others.

The 'Planned / Waitlist Open' status badge on the Brand Designer and Fiction Writer track pages is a confidence-eroding hedge when combined with fully developed module breakdowns. The mismatch between 'this is planned' and three detailed modules with deliverables creates cognitive dissonance — either the course is real enough to have deliverables or it isn't real enough to enroll in.
No track page cross-links to the other specialist tracks. A visitor who arrives at Fiction Writer via search has no path to discover Brand Designer or Copywriter.
strengths: Brand Designer and Fiction Writer track pages are substantively developed: specific problem framing, three modules with named pedagogies, concrete deliverables, and a credibility section (origin/practice). These read as real offerings.
The prerequisite framing on the track pages is direct and honest: 'This track is planned. $699. Requires the full Practitioner Ladder (Courses 01-06).' No ambiguity about the purchase path.

The Brand Designer track problem framing ('the output has the right category but not the right brand') and the Fiction Writer problem framing ('voice has drifted — you can't point to the chapter where it shifted') are specific, practitioner-recognizable situations. These are not generic course descriptions.

The fiction track's 'Where this comes from' section (built inside a real novel, 40+ sessions, kishotenketsu as a lens) significantly strengthens credibility and distinguishes it from a theoretical offering.
Six tracks listed with prices and links is a complete-looking portfolio at the courses.html level, even if back-end pages vary in completeness.

dead_ends: author-track.html exists at /courses/author-track.html but is not linked from courses.html. It is an orphan page — reachable only by direct URL or search index. If indexed by search engines, it will surface stale content (Author track that no longer exists as a named offering) and create confusion. Should be 301-redirected or removed.
courses.html links to copywriter-track.html but this file was not confirmed to exist. If missing, this is a live broken link in the Specialist Tracks section.

Courses 02–06 link to pages that may not exist (input-inversion.html, lens-extraction.html, etc. under /courses/). These are listed as Waitlist but clicking them will 404 if the files haven't been created. Not confirmed here but worth verifying.

The 'Planned / Waitlist Open' specialist track pages all use the same Kit form (9288884) as Foundations enrollment. A visitor cannot distinguish between joining a general list and joining a specific track waitlist — both actions send to the same form.
strengths: Homepage → courses.html is clearly signposted: the nav, the offer card ('See the full course' / 'browse the curriculum'), and the domain-grid 'See the full curriculum' text link all route there.
courses.html → foundations.html is unambiguous: the 01 card is the only 'Enrolling' card, and the header copy explicitly names Foundations as the start.
foundations.html → courses.html is present in the 'What's included' section ('See the full curriculum') with a natural narrative lead-in. The certification section also links back.
Track pages → courses.html is present in the prerequisite note at the bottom of each track page.
Every page terminates in a signup section with a clear CTA. No page ends without a next action.
Prelaunch banner is consistent across all pages and always links to #signup.

issues: The Practitioner Ladder cards on mobile render as a stacked single-column list. The numbered sequence is legible (01, 02, 03 visible in order) but the visual container structure (top cap, cards, bottom cap) is harder to read as a unified bracket — the caps blend into the cards without the horizontal whitespace that makes them distinct on desktop.

The 'After the Ladder' specialist tracks section on mobile renders as a dense text list. The ladder-row layout collapses to stacked text with the $699 price tag appearing at the end of each block. On small screens the price is visually disconnected from the track name at the start of the row.

The prelaunch banner on mobile appears to contain the full pricing string ('Foundations (Course 01) first 50 enroll May 2026. Self-paced. $299 ($499 after). Join the list.') which on very small viewports may wrap awkwardly or compete with the nav.

No CTA is above the fold on mobile for courses.html — the banner is present but the first actionable signup is at the bottom. The 'Get on the list' in the banner is there but its link-affordance may be weak at small size.

priority_fixes.action: Add a 301 redirect from /courses/author-track.html to /courses.html, or delete the file. If search-indexed, stale content will surface for visitors and create a dead end.

priority_fixes.issue: Confirm copywriter-track.html and courses 02–06 pages exist
priority_fixes.action: Verify all linked course pages exist as files. Any missing file is a live broken link in the main curriculum listing. Stub pages with 'Waitlist Open' content and the signup form are sufficient to prevent 404s.

Standardize to one term across all contexts: course cards on courses.html ('Waitlist'), specialist track listing ('Planned / Waitlist Open'), and specialist track pages ('Planned / Waitlist Open'). Either consolidate to 'Waitlist Open' everywhere, or use the two-term version and add a one-sentence note explaining what joining the list means.

priority_fixes.issue: Prerequisite gatekeeping undersold at listing level
priority_fixes.action: On courses.html, add a brief note in the Specialist Tracks section subhead that the prerequisite is a hard gate, not a recommendation. Current phrasing 'Requires the full ladder' is present but visually light. Consider a small visual separator or stronger typographic weight to distinguish it from the track descriptions.

priority_fixes.issue: Creative Director and Educator track descriptions are placeholder-tier
priority_fixes.action: Both tracks get one sentence of description in the courses.html listing, compared to two to three sentences for the other four tracks. Expand to match — or flag them explicitly as 'coming soon' so the disparity reads as intentional sequencing rather than incompleteness.

priority_fixes.issue: Track pages do not cross-link to other tracks
priority_fixes.action: Add a minimal 'Other specialist tracks' navigation element to each track page. Visitors arriving from search have no path to discover the other tracks. A simple row of linked names after the prerequisite note is sufficient.

priority_fixes.issue: All waitlist forms route to the same Kit form
priority_fixes.action: If Kit supports tagged form submissions or multiple forms, differentiate by track so waitlist data is segmentable. If not feasible now, add a hidden field or note in the form placeholder text clarifying which list the visitor is joining ('Brand Designer Track waitlist').
